content.text: for voice AI maintains a persistent, bidirectional connection — typically a WebSocket — that streams audio in both directions simultaneously. This contrasts sharply with REST-style APIs where you upload a complete audio file and wait for a complete response. The persistent connection enables three capabilities that REST cannot:
(server sends audio while receiving it), and
(the server signals when the user has finished speaking, eliminating client-side silence detection).

The major realtime voice APIs in 2025 are OpenAI Realtime API (end-to-end audio LM), Deepgram Streaming (high-accuracy ASR component), and ElevenLabs Conversational AI (agent + TTS-focused). Each fits a different role in the voice-agent stack.

All realtime voice APIs share the same fundamental pattern: the client opens a WebSocket, sends configuration events and audio frames, and receives back a stream of server events. The sequence below shows the OpenAI Realtime API — the most full-featured example:

attrs.alt: OpenAI Realtime API WebSocket event sequence diagram: connect → session.created → session.update → audio streaming with VAD events → response audio deltas → response.done → interruption handling

content.text: Blue arrows flow client→server; green/purple arrows flow server→client. The VAD phase and the response-streaming phase can overlap — the server will fire
mid-response if the user interrupts, and the client must immediately stop playback.

The OpenAI Realtime API (available since October 2024) exposes GPT-4o's native multimodal capability over a persistent WebSocket. Unlike the cascade pattern, the model processes raw audio tokens directly — there is no intermediate ASR transcript unless you opt into the audio-transcript event stream. All audio is 24 kHz mono PCM16 (or G.711 for telephony), base64-encoded in JSON event payloads.
After the connection is established, send a
event to configure audio format, voice, turn detection, system prompt, and any tools the model can call:

import json, websockets, asyncio

REALTIME_URL = (
    "wss://api.openai.com/v1/realtime"
    "?model=gpt-4o-realtime-preview-2024-10-01"
)
HEADERS = {
    "Authorization": "Bearer <OPENAI_API_KEY>",
    "OpenAI-Beta": "realtime=v1",
}

SESSION_CONFIG = {
    "type": "session.update",
    "session": {
        # Audio I/O format — pcm16 (24 kHz mono) or g711_ulaw / g711_alaw
        "input_audio_format": "pcm16",
        "output_audio_format": "pcm16",
        # One of: alloy, echo, fable, onyx, nova, shimmer
        "voice": "alloy",
        # Modalities the model may use in its response
        "modalities": ["audio", "text"],
        # System prompt (instructions)
        "instructions": "You are a helpful voice assistant. Be concise.",
        # Turn detection: server decides when the user stops speaking
        "turn_detection": {
            "type": "server_vad",
            "threshold": 0.5,          # VAD sensitivity 0–1
            "prefix_padding_ms": 300,  # audio before speech start to include
            "silence_duration_ms": 500 # silence needed to end a turn
        },
        # Optional: cap token usage
        "max_response_output_tokens": 1024,
    }
}

async def connect():
    async with websockets.connect(REALTIME_URL,
                                  additional_headers=HEADERS) as ws:
        event = json.loads(await ws.recv())  # session.created
        print("Session:", event["session"]["id"])
        await ws.send(json.dumps(SESSION_CONFIG))
        event = json.loads(await ws.recv())  # session.updated
        return ws
Audio is sent as a continuous stream of

events. The payload is raw PCM16 bytes, base64-encoded. Chunk size is flexible — 20 ms (960 bytes at 24 kHz) to 100 ms are typical. Smaller chunks reduce latency; larger chunks reduce WebSocket overhead.

import base64, pyaudio

CHUNK_MS = 20
SAMPLE_RATE = 24000
CHANNELS = 1
CHUNK_FRAMES = SAMPLE_RATE * CHUNK_MS // 1000  # 480 frames per 20 ms

async def stream_microphone(ws):
    """Capture microphone and push PCM16 chunks to the Realtime API."""
    pa = pyaudio.PyAudio()
    stream = pa.open(
        format=pyaudio.paInt16,
        channels=CHANNELS,
        rate=SAMPLE_RATE,
        input=True,
        frames_per_buffer=CHUNK_FRAMES,
    )
    print("Listening… (Ctrl+C to stop)")
    try:
        while True:
            pcm = stream.read(CHUNK_FRAMES, exception_on_overflow=False)
            await ws.send(json.dumps({
                "type": "input_audio_buffer.append",
                "audio": base64.b64encode(pcm).decode(),
            }))
    finally:
        stream.stop_stream()
        stream.close()
        pa.terminate()
The session can use either server-managed VAD or manual turn control:
Server detects speech start/stop; auto-commits the buffer and triggers a response when silence is detected
Default for most voice apps; no client VAD required
Client commits the buffer explicitly with input_audio_buffer.commit and triggers response with response.create
Push-to-talk UIs, telephony IVR, or when client-side VAD is preferred
In manual mode, you control exactly when the model generates a response. This is essential for push-to-talk UIs or when integrating your own VAD:
The server sends a rich stream of events during response generation. Your event loop must handle all of them:
— response object is initialised; useful for UI "thinking" state.
— contains a base64 PCM16 chunk to play immediately. Arrives many times per response.
— text transcript of what the model is saying, streamed in real time. Useful for captions or logging.
— all audio for this response has been sent. Safe to append a silence buffer.
— streamed JSON arguments when the model calls a tool. Accumulate until
The model can call tools mid-conversation — for example, looking up weather or booking a reservation — without leaving the WebSocket session. Register tools in the session config, then handle
events and submit the result with a

Deepgram's streaming WebSocket is purpose-built for ASR only — it is the highest-accuracy, lowest-latency speech recogniser in a cascade architecture. It supports interim (partial) transcripts that arrive while the user is still speaking, enabling the downstream LLM to begin generating before the utterance completes.

# pip install deepgram-sdk
from deepgram import DeepgramClient, LiveOptions, LiveTranscriptionEvents
import asyncio

async def deepgram_asr(audio_gen, on_final):
    """
    audio_gen: async generator of raw PCM16 bytes (16 kHz mono)
    on_final:  async callback(text: str) called for each completed utterance
    """
    dg = DeepgramClient(api_key="<DG_API_KEY>")
    opts = LiveOptions(
        model="nova-2",
        language="en-US",
        smart_format=True,
        interim_results=True,       # stream partials while user speaks
        utterance_end_ms=1000,      # silence threshold for final utterance
        vad_events=True,            # emit SpeechStarted / UtteranceEnd
    )
    conn = await dg.listen.asynclive.v("1").start(opts)

    # SpeechFinal fires when is_final=True AND speech_final=True
    conn.on(
        LiveTranscriptionEvents.SpeechFinal,
        lambda _, result: asyncio.ensure_future(
            on_final(result.channel.alternatives[0].transcript)
        )
    )

    async for chunk in audio_gen:
        await conn.send(chunk)

    await conn.finish()
    await conn.wait_closed()
Deepgram Nova-2 achieves 3–5% WER on general English and supports 30+ languages. For telephony use cases, set
to match G.711 PSTN audio streams from Twilio or Vonage.

ElevenLabs' Conversational AI API provides a full agent-in-a-box solution: it wraps STT (Deepgram), an LLM (configurable), and ElevenLabs TTS into a single WebSocket session. You define the agent personality, voice, and system prompt in a dashboard or via API, then connect your audio stream and let the platform handle the pipeline. This trades flexibility for ease of integration.

# pip install elevenlabs
from elevenlabs.client import ElevenLabs
from elevenlabs.conversational_ai.conversation import Conversation
from elevenlabs.conversational_ai.default_audio_interface import DefaultAudioInterface

client = ElevenLabs(api_key="<XI_API_KEY>")

# Agent is pre-configured in the ElevenLabs dashboard:
# - voice, system prompt, LLM model, language, etc.
AGENT_ID = "<your_agent_id>"

conversation = Conversation(
    client=client,
    agent_id=AGENT_ID,
    requires_auth=False,     # set True if the agent is private
    audio_interface=DefaultAudioInterface(),  # uses system mic & speaker
    callback_agent_response=lambda text: print(f"Agent: {text}"),
    callback_user_transcript=lambda text: print(f"User: {text}"),
)

conversation.start_session()
conversation.wait_for_session_end()

Browser microphone access goes through the WebRTC stack, which outputs Opus-encoded audio. When connecting a browser to the OpenAI Realtime API you have two options: proxy through your server (server decodes Opus → sends PCM to OpenAI) or use OpenAI's WebRTC beta endpoint that accepts the browser directly. The server-proxy approach is simpler for authentication:

When connecting to PSTN telephone networks via Twilio or Vonage, the audio format is G.711 mulaw (or alaw) at 8 kHz — not PCM16 at 24 kHz. The OpenAI Realtime API supports this natively, avoiding the need for server-side transcoding:

WebSocket connections drop under flaky networks. A production voice agent must detect disconnections and reconnect with exponential backoff while preserving conversation context. The server maintains conversation history per session ID — reconnecting to the same session resumes context:

import asyncio, websockets, json

async def resilient_session(configure_fn, event_handler_fn):
    """Connects, configures, runs, and reconnects on error."""
    backoff = 1
    while True:
        try:
            async with websockets.connect(
                REALTIME_URL,
                additional_headers=HEADERS,
                ping_interval=20,   # keepalive pings
                ping_timeout=10,
            ) as ws:
                await configure_fn(ws)
                backoff = 1  # reset on successful connect
                async for raw in ws:
                    event = json.loads(raw)
                    if event["type"] == "error":
                        print(f"API error: {event['error']['code']} — "
                              f"{event['error']['message']}")
                        # rate_limit_exceeded → back off; others may be fatal
                        if event["error"]["code"] == "rate_limit_exceeded":
                            await asyncio.sleep(backoff)
                            break
                    else:
                        await event_handler_fn(ws, event)

        except (websockets.ConnectionClosed,
                ConnectionResetError,
                OSError) as exc:
            print(f"Connection lost: {exc}. Reconnecting in {backoff}s…")
            await asyncio.sleep(backoff)
            backoff = min(backoff * 2, 32)
The three major realtime APIs serve different roles in the voice-agent stack:
End-to-end LM: STT + LLM + TTS in one model
Interim results; best-in-class WER; per-minute pricing
Full pipeline: STT + LLM + TTS managed together
Easiest to integrate; best TTS voice quality; LLM choice limited
TTS only — streaming synthesis cascade component
SSE streaming; lowest TTS latency; pairs with any LLM
Realtime audio APIs charge on audio tokens (not character count), which changes cost calculations significantly:
— OpenAI Realtime charges roughly 100× more per audio token than per text token. A 60-second call with 30 s of user speech and 30 s of response costs approximately $0.06–0.12 at 2024 pricing.

— set this in the session config to cap the model's response length. Without a cap, a verbose response runs until the model decides to stop. For voice, 150–300 output audio tokens (≈ 10–20 seconds of speech) is often enough.
— Tier 1 OpenAI accounts allow ~3 concurrent Realtime sessions; Tier 5 allows hundreds. Plan capacity accordingly for concurrent-user scenarios.
— the input audio buffer charges for all audio sent, including silence frames. Use client-side VAD to gate sends: only push audio when voice is detected, not continuously.
Realtime voice APIs maintain persistent WebSocket connections that stream audio bidirectionally, enabling sub-300 ms latency and full-duplex operation that REST APIs cannot match.
The OpenAI Realtime API uses an event-driven model: the client sends
automatically, triggering the response stream of
(manual) for push-to-talk UIs or telephony where you control the turn boundary explicitly with

G.711 mulaw support in the OpenAI Realtime API enables direct Twilio/telephony integration without server-side transcoding — forward the raw mulaw payload from Twilio's Media Stream WebSocket directly as
Audio tokens cost roughly 100× more than text tokens — gate microphone sends with client-side VAD and set
to control costs; build exponential-backoff reconnection logic since WebSocket connections drop in production.
